content.assessment: Quotas and outreach attempt to change who is selected into existing tracks without altering the tracking mechanism or timing, directly addressing compositional imbalance. However, evidence on value-added gains being substantially attributable to family background rather than institutional effect, plus a reported disappearing long-run labor-market benefit of track selection, raises real uncertainty about whether quota-admitted disadvantaged students would durably benefit — this is a genuinely contested point in the evidence. Starting outreach before the age-10 selection point aligns with findings that earlier, kindergarten-stage interventions produce stronger equity gains for low-status children.
opportunities: Could be combined with kindergarten-stage outreach given evidence that earlier intervention yields stronger equity returns for low-SES children
strengths: Directly targets the compositional imbalance (documented disparity between advantaged and deprived representation among top performers)
Addresses financing-based access gap (tutoring) without requiring exam-content or structural redesign
Aligns with evidence that earlier interventions (pre-age-10) produce stronger equity gains for low-status children than later ones
threats: Quotas may not shift underlying parental sorting behavior or distrust of local non-selective schools driving demand for elite tracks
Risk of tokenistic inclusion without addressing broader school-quality disparities feeding segregation
weaknesses: Contested evidence on whether track value-added is genuine institutional effect or family-background confound undermines certainty that quota-admitted students gain durable benefit
Reported disappearance of track selection's long-run labor-market effect raises doubt about downstream mobility gains from expanded access alone
Leaves the early tracking mechanism itself unaltered, so structural sorting risk for non-quota students remains
